(Ἑλληνική, *Hellēnikḗ* [[hellɛːnikɛ́ː]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Greek "Help:IPA/Greek"))[[1]](#cite_note-1) includes the forms of the [Greek language](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language") used in [ancient Greece](/wiki/Ancient_Greece "Ancient Greece") and the [ancient world](/wiki/Classical_antiquity "Classical antiquity") from around 1500 BC to 300 BC. It is often roughly divided into the following periods: [Mycenaean Greek](/wiki/Mycenaean_Greek "Mycenaean Greek") (c. 1400 – c. 1200 BC), [Dark Ages](/wiki/Greek_Dark_Ages "Greek Dark Ages") (c. 1200 – c. 800 BC), the [Archaic](/wiki/Archaic_Greece "Archaic Greece") or [Homeric](/wiki/Homeric_Greek "Homeric Greek") period (c. 800 – c. 500 BC), and the [Classical](/wiki/Classical_Greece "Classical Greece") period (c. 500 – c. 300 BC).[[2]](#cite_note-2)

group: * [Doric proper](/wiki/Doric_Greek#Doric_proper "Doric Greek") * [Northwest Doric](/wiki/Doric_Greek#Northwest_Doric "Doric Greek") * [Achaean Doric](/wiki/Achaean_Doric_Greek "Achaean Doric Greek") (probably Northwest Doric) | **Central** group: * [Aeolic](/wiki/Aeolic_Greek "Aeolic Greek") * [Arcado-Cypriot](/wiki/Arcadocypriot_Greek "Arcadocypriot Greek") | **Eastern** group: * [Attic](/wiki/Attic_Greek "Attic Greek") * [Ionic](/wiki/Ionic_Greek "Ionic Greek") |

**Ancient Greek** (Ἑλληνική, *Hellēnikḗ* [[hellɛːnikɛ́ː]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Greek "Help:IPA/Greek"))[[1]](#cite_note-1) includes the forms of the [Greek language](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language") used in [ancient Greece](/wiki/Ancient_Greece "Ancient Greece") and the [ancient world](/wiki/Classical_antiquity "Classical antiquity") from around 1500 BC to 300 BC. It is often roughly divided into the following periods: [Mycenaean Greek](/wiki/Mycenaean_Greek "Mycenaean Greek") (c. 1400 – c. 1200 BC), [Dark Ages](/wiki/Greek_Dark_Ages "Greek Dark Ages") (c. 1200 – c. 800 BC), the [Archaic](/wiki/Archaic_Greece "Archaic Greece") or [Homeric](/wiki/Homeric_Greek "Homeric Greek") period (c. 800 – c. 500 BC), and the [Classical](/wiki/Classical_Greece "Classical Greece") period (c. 500 – c. 300 BC).[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Ancient Greek was the language of [Homer](/wiki/Homer "Homer") and of [fifth-century Athenian](/wiki/Fifth-century_Athens "Fifth-century Athens") historians, playwrights, and [philosophers](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_philosophy "Ancient Greek philosophy"). It has contributed many words to English vocabulary and has been a standard subject of study in educational institutions of the [Western world](/wiki/Western_world "Western world") since the [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance"). This article primarily contains information about the [Epic](/wiki/Homeric_Greek "Homeric Greek") and Classical periods of the language, which are the best-attested periods and considered most typical of Ancient Greek.

From the [Hellenistic period](/wiki/Hellenistic_period "Hellenistic period") (c. 300 BC), Ancient Greek was followed by [Koine Greek](/wiki/Koine_Greek "Koine Greek"), which is regarded as a separate historical stage, though its earliest form closely resembles [Attic Greek](/wiki/Attic_Greek "Attic Greek"), and its latest form approaches [Medieval Greek](/wiki/Medieval_Greek "Medieval Greek"), and Koine may be classified as Ancient Greek in a wider sense – being an ancient rather than medieval form of Greek, though over the centuries increasingly resembling Medieval and [Modern Greek](/wiki/Modern_Greek "Modern Greek").

Ancient Greek comprised several [dialects](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_dialects "Ancient Greek dialects"), such as Attic, [Ionic](/wiki/Ionic_Greek "Ionic Greek"), [Doric](/wiki/Doric_Greek "Doric Greek"), [Aeolic](/wiki/Aeolic "Aeolic"), and [Arcadocypriot](/wiki/Arcadocypriot "Arcadocypriot"); among them, Attic Greek became the basis of Koine Greek. Just like Koine is often included in Ancient Greek, conversely, Mycenaean Greek is usually treated separately and not always included in Ancient Greek – reflecting the fact that Greek in the first millennium BC is considered prototypical of Ancient Greek.

## Dialects

Main article: [Ancient Greek dialects](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_dialects "Ancient Greek dialects")

Ancient Greek was a [pluricentric language](/wiki/Pluricentric_language "Pluricentric language"), divided into many dialects. The main dialect groups are [Attic](/wiki/Attic_Greek "Attic Greek") and [Ionic](/wiki/Ionic_Greek "Ionic Greek"), [Aeolic](/wiki/Aeolic_Greek "Aeolic Greek"), [Arcadocypriot](/wiki/Arcadocypriot_Greek "Arcadocypriot Greek"), and [Doric](/wiki/Doric_Greek "Doric Greek"), many of them with several subdivisions. Some dialects are found in standardized literary forms in [literature](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_literature "Ancient Greek literature"), while others are attested only in inscriptions.

There are also several historical forms. [Homeric Greek](/wiki/Homeric_Greek "Homeric Greek") is a literary form of Archaic Greek (derived primarily from Ionic and Aeolic) used in the [epic poems](/wiki/Epic_poetry "Epic poetry"), the *[Iliad](/wiki/Iliad "Iliad")* and the *[Odyssey](/wiki/Odyssey "Odyssey")*, and in later poems by other authors.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Homeric Greek had significant differences in grammar and pronunciation from Classical Attic and other Classical-era dialects.

### History

The origins, early form and development of the Hellenic language family are not well understood because of a lack of contemporaneous evidence. Several theories exist about what Hellenic dialect groups may have existed between the divergence of early Greek-like speech from the common [Proto-Indo-European language](/wiki/Proto-Indo-European_language "Proto-Indo-European language") and the Classical period. They have the same general outline but differ in some of the detail. The only attested dialect from this period[[a]](#cite_note-4) is [Mycenaean Greek](/wiki/Mycenaean_Greek "Mycenaean Greek"), but its relationship to the historical dialects and the historical circumstances of the times imply that the overall groups already existed in some form.

Scholars assume that major Ancient Greek period dialect groups developed no later than 1120 BC, at the time of the [Dorian invasions](/wiki/Dorian_invasion "Dorian invasion")—and that their first appearances as precise alphabetic writing began in the 8th century BC. The invasion would not be "Dorian" unless the invaders had some cultural relationship to the historical [Dorians](/wiki/Dorians "Dorians"). The invasion is known to have displaced population to the later Attic-Ionic regions, who regarded themselves as descendants of the population displaced by or contending with the Dorians.

The Greeks of this period believed there were three major divisions of all Greek people – Dorians, Aeolians, and Ionians (including Athenians), each with their own defining and distinctive dialects. Allowing for their oversight of Arcadian, an obscure mountain dialect, and Cypriot, far from the center of Greek scholarship, this division of people and language is quite similar to the results of modern archaeological-linguistic investigation.

West vs. non-West Greek is the strongest-marked and earliest division, with non-West in subsets of Ionic-Attic (or Attic-Ionic) and Aeolic vs. Arcadocypriot, or Aeolic and Arcado-Cypriot vs. Ionic-Attic. Often non-West is called 'East Greek'.

Arcadocypriot apparently descended more closely from the Mycenaean Greek of the Bronze Age.

[Boeotian Greek](/wiki/Boeotian_Greek "Boeotian Greek") had come under a strong Northwest Greek influence, and can in some respects be considered a transitional dialect, as exemplified in the poems of the [Boeotian](/wiki/Boeotia "Boeotia") poet [Pindar](/wiki/Pindar "Pindar") who wrote in Doric with a small Aeolic admixture.[[6]](#cite_note-7) [Thessalian](/wiki/Thessalian "Thessalian") likewise had come under Northwest Greek influence, though to a lesser degree.

[Pamphylian Greek](/wiki/Pamphylian_Greek "Pamphylian Greek"), spoken in a small area on the southwestern coast of Anatolia and little preserved in inscriptions, may be either a fifth major dialect group, or it is Mycenaean Greek overlaid by Doric, with a non-Greek native influence.[[7]](#cite_note-8)

Regarding the speech of the [ancient Macedonians](/wiki/Ancient_Macedonians "Ancient Macedonians") diverse theories have been put forward, but the epigraphic activity and the archaeological discoveries in the [Greek region of Macedonia](/wiki/Macedonia_(Greece) "Macedonia (Greece)") during the last decades has brought to light documents, among which the first texts written in [Macedonian](/wiki/Ancient_Macedonian_language "Ancient Macedonian language"), such as the [Pella curse tablet](/wiki/Pella_curse_tablet "Pella curse tablet"), as Hatzopoulos and other scholars note.[[8]](#cite_note-Hornblower2002-9)[[9]](#cite_note-Hatzopoulos2018-10) Based on the conclusions drawn by several studies and findings such as [Pella curse tablet](/wiki/Pella_curse_tablet "Pella curse tablet"), Emilio Crespo and most scholars suggest that [ancient Macedonian](/wiki/Ancient_Macedonian_dialect "Ancient Macedonian dialect") was a [Northwest Doric dialect](/wiki/Doric_Greek "Doric Greek"),[[b]](#cite_note-18) which shares isoglosses with its neighboring [Thessalian dialects](/wiki/Thessalian_Greek "Thessalian Greek") spoken in northeastern [Thessaly](/wiki/Ancient_Thessaly "Ancient Thessaly").[[17]](#cite_note-Crespo2018-19)[[9]](#cite_note-Hatzopoulos2018-10) Some have also suggested an [Aeolic Greek](/wiki/Aeolic_Greek "Aeolic Greek") classification.[[18]](#cite_note-20)[[19]](#cite_note-21)

The [Lesbian](/wiki/Lesbos "Lesbos") dialect was Aeolic. For example, fragments of the works of the poet [Sappho](/wiki/Sappho "Sappho") from the island of [Lesbos](/wiki/Lesbos "Lesbos") are in Aeolian.[[20]](#cite_note-22)

Most of the dialect sub-groups listed above had further subdivisions, generally equivalent to a city-state and its surrounding territory, or to an island. Doric notably had several intermediate divisions as well, into Island Doric (including [Cretan Doric](/wiki/Doric_Greek#Cretan "Doric Greek")), Southern Peloponnesus Doric (including [Laconian](/wiki/Doric_Greek#Laconian "Doric Greek"), the dialect of [Sparta](/wiki/Sparta "Sparta")), and Northern Peloponnesus Doric (including [Corinthian](/wiki/Doric_Greek#Corinthian "Doric Greek")).

All the groups were represented by colonies beyond Greece proper as well, and these colonies generally developed local characteristics, often under the influence of settlers or neighbors speaking different Greek dialects.

After the conquests of [Alexander the Great](/wiki/Alexander_the_Great "Alexander the Great") in the late 4th century BC, a new international dialect known as [Koine](/wiki/Koine_Greek "Koine Greek") or Common Greek developed, largely based on [Attic Greek](/wiki/Attic_Greek "Attic Greek"), but with influence from other dialects. This dialect slowly replaced most of the older dialects, although the Doric dialect has survived in the [Tsakonian language](/wiki/Tsakonian_language "Tsakonian language"), which is spoken in the region of modern Sparta. Doric has also passed down its aorist terminations into most verbs of [Demotic Greek](/wiki/Demotic_Greek "Demotic Greek"). By about the 6th century AD, the Koine had slowly metamorphosed into [Medieval Greek](/wiki/Medieval_Greek "Medieval Greek").

### Related languages

Main article: [Phrygian language](/wiki/Phrygian_language "Phrygian language")

[Phrygian](/wiki/Phrygian_language "Phrygian language") is an extinct [Indo-European](/wiki/Indo-European_languages "Indo-European languages") language of West and Central [Anatolia](/wiki/Anatolia "Anatolia"), which is regarded by current consensus to have been closely related to [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language").[[21]](#cite_note-Brixhe_pp._165–178-23)[[22]](#cite_note-24)[[23]](#cite_note-25) Among Indo-European branches with living descendants, Greek is often argued to have the closest genetic ties with [Armenian](/wiki/Armenian_language "Armenian language")[[24]](#cite_note-26) (see also [Graeco-Armenian](/wiki/Graeco-Armenian "Graeco-Armenian")) and [Indo-Iranian languages](/wiki/Indo-Iranian_languages "Indo-Iranian languages") (see [Graeco-Aryan](/wiki/Graeco-Aryan "Graeco-Aryan")).[[25]](#cite_note-27)[[26]](#cite_note-28)

## Phonology

### Differences from Proto-Indo-European

Main article: [Proto-Greek language](/wiki/Proto-Greek_language "Proto-Greek language")

Ancient Greek differs from [Proto-Indo-European](/wiki/Proto-Indo-European_language "Proto-Indo-European language") (PIE) and other Indo-European languages in certain ways. In [phonotactics](/wiki/Phonotactics "Phonotactics"), ancient Greek words could end only in a vowel or /n s r/; final stops were lost, as in γάλα "milk", compared with γάλακτος "of milk" (genitive). Ancient Greek of the classical period also differed in both the inventory and distribution of original PIE phonemes due to numerous sound changes,[[27]](#cite_note-Fortson-29) notably the following:

* PIE \**s* became /h/ at the beginning of a word ([debuccalization](/wiki/Debuccalization "Debuccalization")): Latin **sex**, English *six*, ancient Greek ἕξ /héks/.
* PIE \**s* was [elided](/wiki/Elision "Elision") between vowels after an intermediate step of debuccalization: Sanskrit **janasas**, Latin **generis** (where *s* > *r* by [rhotacism](/wiki/Rhotacism_(sound_change) "Rhotacism (sound change)")), Greek \**genesos* > \**genehos* > ancient Greek γένεος (/ɡéneos/), Attic γένους (/ɡénoːs/) "of a kind".
* PIE \**y* /j/ became /h/ (debuccalization) or /(d)z/ ([fortition](/wiki/Fortition "Fortition")): Sanskrit **yas**, ancient Greek ὅς /hós/ "who" (relative pronoun); Latin **iugum**, English *yoke*, ancient Greek ζυγός /zyɡós/.
* PIE \**w*, which occurred in [Mycenaean](/wiki/Mycenaean_Greek "Mycenaean Greek") and some non-Attic dialects, was lost: early Doric ϝέργον /wérɡon/, English *work*, Attic Greek ἔργον /érɡon/.
* PIE and Mycenaean labiovelars changed to plain stops (labials, dentals, and velars) in the later Greek dialects: for instance, PIE \**kʷ* became /p/ or /t/ in Attic: Attic Greek ποῦ /pôː/ "where?", Latin **quō**; Attic Greek τίς /tís/, Latin **quis** "who?".
* PIE "voiced aspirated" stops *bʰ dʰ ǵʰ gʰ gʷʰ* were devoiced and became the aspirated stops φ θ χ /pʰ tʰ kʰ/ in ancient Greek.

### Phonemic inventory

Main article: [Ancient Greek phonology](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_phonology "Ancient Greek phonology")

The pronunciation of Ancient Greek was very different from that of [Modern Greek](/wiki/Modern_Greek "Modern Greek"). Ancient Greek had [long and short vowels](/wiki/Vowel_length "Vowel length"); many [diphthongs](/wiki/Diphthong "Diphthong"); [double](/wiki/Gemination "Gemination") and single consonants; voiced, voiceless, and aspirated [stops](/wiki/Stop_consonant "Stop consonant"); and a [pitch accent](/wiki/Pitch_accent "Pitch accent"). In Modern Greek, all vowels and consonants are short. Many vowels and diphthongs once pronounced distinctly are pronounced as /i/ ([iotacism](/wiki/Iotacism "Iotacism")). Some of the stops and [glides](/wiki/Semivowel "Semivowel") in diphthongs have become [fricatives](/wiki/Fricative_consonant "Fricative consonant"), and the pitch accent has changed to a [stress accent](/wiki/Stress_(linguistics) "Stress (linguistics)"). Many of the changes took place in the [Koine Greek](/wiki/Koine_Greek "Koine Greek") period. The writing system of Modern Greek, however, does not reflect all pronunciation changes.

The examples below represent Attic Greek in the 5th century BC. Ancient pronunciation cannot be reconstructed with certainty, but Greek from the period is well documented, and there is little disagreement among linguists as to the general nature of the sounds that the letters represent.

## Morphology

Main article: [Ancient Greek grammar](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_grammar "Ancient Greek grammar")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/AGMA_Ostrakon_Cimon.jpg/250px-AGMA_Ostrakon_Cimon.jpg)](/wiki/File:AGMA_Ostrakon_Cimon.jpg)

[Ostracon](/wiki/Ostracon "Ostracon") bearing the name of [Cimon](/wiki/Cimon "Cimon"), [Stoa of Attalos](/wiki/Stoa_of_Attalos "Stoa of Attalos")

Greek, like all of the older [Indo-European languages](/wiki/Indo-European_languages "Indo-European languages"), is highly inflected. It is highly archaic in its preservation of [Proto-Indo-European](/wiki/Proto-Indo-European_language "Proto-Indo-European language") forms. In ancient Greek, [nouns](/wiki/Noun "Noun") (including proper nouns) have five [cases](/wiki/Declension "Declension") ([nominative](/wiki/Nominative_case "Nominative case"), [genitive](/wiki/Genitive_case "Genitive case"), [dative](/wiki/Dative_case "Dative case"), [accusative](/wiki/Accusative_case "Accusative case"), and [vocative](/wiki/Vocative_case "Vocative case")), three [genders](/wiki/Grammatical_gender "Grammatical gender") ([masculine](/wiki/Grammatical_gender "Grammatical gender"), [feminine](/wiki/Grammatical_gender "Grammatical gender"), and [neuter](/wiki/Grammatical_gender "Grammatical gender")), and three [numbers](/wiki/Grammatical_number "Grammatical number") (singular, [dual](/wiki/Dual_(grammatical_number) "Dual (grammatical number)"), and [plural](/wiki/Plural "Plural")). [Verbs](/wiki/Verb "Verb") have four [moods](/wiki/Grammatical_mood "Grammatical mood") ([indicative](/wiki/Realis_mood "Realis mood"), [imperative](/wiki/Imperative_mood "Imperative mood"), [subjunctive](/wiki/Subjunctive_mood "Subjunctive mood"), and [optative](/wiki/Optative_mood "Optative mood")) and three [voices](/wiki/Voice_(grammar) "Voice (grammar)") (active, middle, and [passive](/wiki/English_passive_voice "English passive voice")), as well as three [persons](/wiki/Grammatical_person "Grammatical person") (first, second, and third) and various other forms.

Verbs are conjugated through seven combinations of [tenses](/wiki/Grammatical_tense "Grammatical tense") and [aspect](/wiki/Grammatical_aspect "Grammatical aspect") (generally simply called "tenses"): the [present](/wiki/Present_tense "Present tense"), [future](/wiki/Future_tense "Future tense"), and [imperfect](/wiki/Imperfect "Imperfect") are [imperfective](/wiki/Imperfective_aspect "Imperfective aspect") in aspect; the [aorist](/wiki/Aorist "Aorist"), [present perfect](/wiki/Present_perfect "Present perfect"), [pluperfect](/wiki/Pluperfect "Pluperfect") and [future perfect](/wiki/Future_perfect "Future perfect") are [perfective](/wiki/Perfective_aspect "Perfective aspect") in aspect. Most tenses display all four moods and three voices, although there is no future subjunctive or imperative. Also, there is no imperfect subjunctive, optative or imperative. The infinitives and participles correspond to the finite combinations of tense, aspect, and voice.

### Augment

The indicative of past [tenses](/wiki/Grammatical_tense "Grammatical tense") adds (conceptually, at least) a prefix /e-/, called the [augment](/wiki/Augment_(Indo-European) "Augment (Indo-European)"). This was probably originally a separate word, meaning something like "then", added because tenses in PIE had primarily aspectual meaning. The augment is added to the [indicative](/wiki/Realis_mood "Realis mood") of the aorist, imperfect, and pluperfect, but not to any of the other forms of the aorist (no other forms of the imperfect and pluperfect exist).

The two kinds of augment in Greek are syllabic and quantitative. The syllabic augment is added to stems beginning with consonants, and simply prefixes *e* (stems beginning with *r*, however, add *er*). The quantitative augment is added to stems beginning with vowels, and involves lengthening the vowel:

* a, ā, e, ē → ē
* i, ī → ī
* o, ō → ō
* u, ū → ū
* ai → ēi
* ei → ēi or ei
* oi → ōi
* au → ēu or au
* eu → ēu or eu
* ou → ou

Some verbs augment irregularly; the most common variation is *e* → *ei*. The irregularity can be explained diachronically by the loss of *s* between vowels, or that of the letter *w*, which affected the augment when it was word-initial.
In verbs with a preposition as a prefix, the augment is placed not at the start of the word, but between the preposition and the original verb. For example, προσ(-)βάλλω (I attack) goes to προσ**έ**βαλoν in the aorist. However compound verbs consisting of a prefix that is not a preposition retain the augment at the start of the word: αὐτο(-)μολῶ goes to **ηὐ**τομόλησα in the aorist.

Following [Homer](/wiki/Homer "Homer")'s practice, the augment is sometimes not made in [poetry](/wiki/Poetry "Poetry"), especially [epic](/wiki/Homeric_Greek "Homeric Greek") poetry.

The augment sometimes substitutes for reduplication; see below.

### Reduplication

Almost all forms of the perfect, pluperfect, and future perfect reduplicate the initial syllable of the verb stem. (A few irregular forms of perfect do not reduplicate, whereas a handful of irregular aorists reduplicate.) The three types of [reduplication](/wiki/Reduplication "Reduplication") are:

* Syllabic reduplication: Most verbs beginning with a single consonant, or a cluster of a stop with a sonorant, add a syllable consisting of the initial consonant followed by *e*. An aspirated consonant, however, reduplicates in its unaspirated equivalent (see [Grassmann's law](/wiki/Grassmann%27s_law "Grassmann's law")).
* Augment: Verbs beginning with a vowel, as well as those beginning with a cluster other than those indicated previously (and occasionally for a few other verbs) reduplicate in the same fashion as the augment. This remains in all forms of the perfect, not just the indicative.
* Attic reduplication: Some verbs beginning with an *a*, *e* or *o*, followed by a sonorant (or occasionally *d* or *g*), reduplicate by adding a syllable consisting of the initial vowel and following consonant, and lengthening the following vowel. Hence *er* → *erēr*, *an* → *anēn*, *ol* → *olōl*, *ed* → *edēd*. This is not specific to [Attic Greek](/wiki/Attic_Greek "Attic Greek"), despite its name, but it was generalized in Attic. This originally involved reduplicating a cluster consisting of a [laryngeal](/wiki/Laryngeal_theory "Laryngeal theory") and sonorant, hence **h₃l* → *h₃leh₃l* → *olōl** with normal Greek development of laryngeals. (Forms with a stop were analogous.)

Irregular duplication can be understood diachronically. For example, *lambanō* (root *lab*) has the perfect stem *eilēpha* (not \**lelēpha*) because it was originally *slambanō*, with perfect *seslēpha*, becoming *eilēpha* through compensatory lengthening.

Reduplication is also visible in the present tense stems of certain verbs. These stems add a syllable consisting of the root's initial consonant followed by *i*. A nasal stop appears after the reduplication in some verbs.[[28]](#cite_note-Palmer1996p262-30)

Main article: [Greek orthography](/wiki/Greek_orthography "Greek orthography")

The earliest extant examples of ancient Greek writing (c. 1450 BC) are in the syllabic script [Linear B](/wiki/Linear_B "Linear B"). Beginning in the 8th century BC, however, the [Greek alphabet](/wiki/Greek_alphabet "Greek alphabet") became standard, albeit with some variation among dialects. Early texts are written in [boustrophedon](/wiki/Boustrophedon "Boustrophedon") style, but left-to-right became standard during the classic period. Modern editions of ancient Greek texts are usually written with [accents and breathing marks](/wiki/Greek_diacritics "Greek diacritics"), [interword spacing](/wiki/Word_divider "Word divider"), modern [punctuation](/wiki/Punctuation "Punctuation"), and sometimes [mixed case](/wiki/Capitalization "Capitalization"), but these were all introduced later.

## Sample texts

The beginning of [Homer](/wiki/Homer "Homer")'s [Iliad](/wiki/Iliad "Iliad") exemplifies the Archaic period of ancient Greek (see [Homeric Greek](/wiki/Homeric_Greek "Homeric Greek") for more details):

> Μῆνιν ἄειδε, θεά, Πηληϊάδεω Ἀχιλῆος  
> οὐλομένην, ἣ μυρί' Ἀχαιοῖς ἄλγε' ἔθηκε,  
> πολλὰς δ' ἰφθίμους ψυχὰς Ἄϊδι προΐαψεν  
> ἡρώων, αὐτοὺς δὲ ἑλώρια τεῦχε κύνεσσιν  
> οἰωνοῖσί τε πᾶσι· Διὸς δ' ἐτελείετο βουλή·  
> ἐξ οὗ δὴ τὰ πρῶτα διαστήτην ἐρίσαντε  
> Ἀτρεΐδης τε ἄναξ ἀνδρῶν καὶ δῖος Ἀχιλλεύς.

The beginning of [Apology](/wiki/Apology_(Plato) "Apology (Plato)") by [Plato](/wiki/Plato "Plato") exemplifies [Attic Greek](/wiki/Attic_Greek "Attic Greek") from the Classical period of ancient Greek. (The second line is the [IPA](/wiki/International_Phonetic_Alphabet "International Phonetic Alphabet"), the third is transliterated into the Latin alphabet using a modern version of the [Erasmian scheme](/wiki/Pronunciation_of_Ancient_Greek_in_teaching "Pronunciation of Ancient Greek in teaching").)

## Modern use

See also: [Neoclassical compound](/wiki/Neoclassical_compound "Neoclassical compound")

### In education

The study of Ancient Greek in European countries in addition to [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") occupied an important place in the syllabus from the [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance") until the beginning of the 20th century. This was true as well in the United States, where many of the nation's founders received a classically based education.[[c]](#cite_note-33) Latin was emphasized in American colleges, but Greek also was required in the colonial and early national eras,[[d]](#cite_note-36) and the study of ancient Greece became increasingly popular in the mid-to-late nineteenth century, the age of American [philhellenism](/wiki/Philhellenism "Philhellenism").[[33]](#cite_note-37) In particular, female intellectuals of the era designated the mastering of ancient Greek as essential in becoming a "woman of letters".[[34]](#cite_note-38)

Ancient Greek is still taught as a compulsory or optional subject especially at traditional or elite schools throughout Europe, such as [public schools](/wiki/Independent_school_(UK) "Independent school (UK)") and [grammar schools](/wiki/Grammar_school "Grammar school") in the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"). It is compulsory in the *[liceo classico](/wiki/Liceo_classico "Liceo classico")* in [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy"), in the *gymnasium* in the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands"), in some classes in [Austria](/wiki/Austria "Austria"), in *klasična gimnazija* (grammar school – orientation: classical languages) in [Croatia](/wiki/Croatia "Croatia"), in classical studies in [ASO](/wiki/Education_in_Belgium#Structure "Education in Belgium") in Belgium, and it is optional in the [humanities-oriented *gymnasium*](/wiki/Gymnasium_(Germany)#Humanistisches_Gymnasium_(humanities-oriented) "Gymnasium (Germany)") in [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany"), usually as a third language after Latin and English, from the age of 14 to 18. In 2006/07, 15,000 pupils studied ancient Greek in Germany according to the [Federal Statistical Office of Germany](/wiki/Federal_Statistical_Office_of_Germany "Federal Statistical Office of Germany"), and 280,000 pupils studied it in Italy.[[35]](#cite_note-39)

It is a compulsory subject alongside Latin in the humanities branch of the [Spanish Baccalaureate](/wiki/Spanish_Baccalaureate "Spanish Baccalaureate"). Ancient Greek is taught at most major [universities](/wiki/University "University") worldwide, often combined with [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") as part of the study of [classics](/wiki/Classics "Classics"). In 2010 it was offered in three primary schools in the [UK](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"), to boost children's language skills,[[36]](#cite_note-40)[[37]](#cite_note-41) and was one of seven foreign languages which primary schools could teach 2014 as part of a major drive to boost education standards.[[38]](#cite_note-42)[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*]

Ancient Greek is taught as a compulsory subject in all [gymnasiums](/wiki/Gymnasium_(school) "Gymnasium (school)") and [lyceums](/wiki/Lyceum "Lyceum") in [Greece](/wiki/Greece "Greece").[[39]](#cite_note-43)[[40]](#cite_note-44) Starting in 2001, an annual international competition "Exploring the Ancient Greek Language and Culture" ([Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language"): Διαγωνισμός στην Αρχαία Ελληνική Γλώσσα και Γραμματεία) was run for upper secondary students through the Greek [Ministry of National Education and Religious Affairs](/wiki/Ministry_of_National_Education_and_Religious_Affairs "Ministry of National Education and Religious Affairs"), with Greek language and cultural organisations as co-organisers.[[41]](#cite_note-45) It appears to have ceased in 2010, having failed to gain the recognition and acceptance of teachers.[[42]](#cite_note-46)[*[full citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#What_information_to_include "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

### Modern real-world usage

Modern authors rarely write in ancient Greek, though [Jan Křesadlo](/wiki/Jan_K%C5%99esadlo "Jan Křesadlo") wrote some poetry and prose in the language, and *[Harry Potter and the Philosopher's Stone](/wiki/Harry_Potter_and_the_Philosopher%27s_Stone "Harry Potter and the Philosopher's Stone")*,[[43]](#cite_note-47) [some volumes](/wiki/Asterix#Translations "Asterix") of [Asterix](/wiki/Asterix "Asterix"),[[44]](#cite_note-48) and *[The Adventures of Alix](/wiki/The_Adventures_of_Alix "The Adventures of Alix")* have been translated into ancient Greek. Ὀνόματα Kεχιασμένα (*[Onomata Kechiasmena](/wiki/Onomata_Kechiasmena "Onomata Kechiasmena")*) is the first magazine of crosswords and puzzles in ancient Greek.[[45]](#cite_note-49) Its first issue appeared in April 2015 as an annex to *[Hebdomada Aenigmatum](/wiki/Hebdomada_Aenigmatum "Hebdomada Aenigmatum")*. [Alfred Rahlfs](/wiki/Alfred_Rahlfs "Alfred Rahlfs") included a preface, a short history of the [Septuagint](/wiki/Septuagint "Septuagint") text, and other [front matter](/wiki/Front_matter "Front matter") translated into ancient Greek in his 1935 edition of the Septuagint; Robert Hanhart also included the introductory remarks to the 2006 revised Rahlfs–Hanhart edition in the language as well.[[46]](#cite_note-50) *Akropolis World News* reports weekly a summary of the most important news in ancient Greek.[[47]](#cite_note-akropolis-51)

Ancient Greek is also used by organizations and individuals, mainly Greek, who wish to denote their respect, admiration or preference for the use of this language. This use is sometimes considered graphical, nationalistic or humorous. In any case, the fact that modern Greeks can still wholly or partly understand texts written in non-archaic forms of ancient Greek shows the affinity of the modern Greek language to its ancestral predecessor.[[47]](#cite_note-akropolis-51)

Ancient Greek is often used in the coinage of modern technical terms in the European languages: see [English words of Greek origin](/wiki/English_words_of_Greek_origin "English words of Greek origin"). [Latinized](/wiki/Latinisation_of_names "Latinisation of names") forms of ancient Greek roots are used in many of the [scientific names](/wiki/Binomial_nomenclature "Binomial nomenclature") of [species](/wiki/Species "Species") and in scientific terminology.
